utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is significantly more effective than wo_skills. The wi_skills run produced a comprehensive retainage package with: (1) a formal dual-tier owner vs. sub retention breakdown with explicit net position; (2) a detailed per-subcontractor release condition checklist covering 7 distinct categories; (3) a complete aged retention analysis table with days-held computed from actual billing dates and rankings; and (4) a richly structured JSON with full release-readiness metadata including per-condition status flags. The wo_skills run produced correct balance figures and correctly handled prior releases, but failed to compute the net retention position formally, omitted aged retention analysis entirely, and only evaluated 2 release conditions per subcontractor instead of the required 4+. The skill-enabled run demonstrates clear superiority in completeness and analytical depth.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is significantly more effective than wo_skills. wi_skills correctly calculated 50% release amounts for all 5 subcontractors based on actual cumulative billing history, documented lien waiver and surety status per sub, computed accurate post-release remaining balances, produced a properly disaggregated 5-entry cashflow forecast, read the release schedule from the input file, and tracked partial_release status transitions. The wi_skills run encountered a minor issue where pandas was not available and pip was not found, requiring the agent to rewrite the script to use the built-in csv module, but the script ultimately succeeded.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality outputs that pass all verifier checks and satisfy most evaluation criteria. Both correctly apply variable retention rates, identify both billing irregularities with quantified discrepancies, compute the net GC retention cash position, differentiate completion statuses, and produce board-readable structured reports. The key differentiator is JE-U3-03: the wo_skills report more explicitly itemizes specific missing documentation blockers per final-completion sub (warranty docs for SUB-4; as-builts and consent of surety for SUB-6) in the report body, whereas the wi_skills report's visible sections are slightly less explicit about specific missing items per sub. Neither run includes aged-retention with days-held for active subs (JE-U3-06). Overall, the two runs are nearly equivalent in quality, with wo_skills having a marginal edge in documentation specificity for JE-U3-03, but wi_skills leveraged the skill infrastructure more systematically. The outputs are comparable, with wo_skills arguably producing a slightly more detailed and better-structured final report.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both runs produced valid outputs passing all 5 verifier checks, but they differ in quality on specific evaluation items. wi_skills correctly applied variable retention rates and used the skill's structured ledger to compute per-subcontractor balances at the right rates (SUB-4 and SUB-6 at 5%), but failed JE-U3-05 by only showing 2 statuses ('held' and 'partial_release') instead of distinguishing final-completion subs with 'pending_release', and failed JE-U3-06 by not surfacing aged retention with days-held. wo_skills correctly identified variable rates, both billing irregularities, per-sub documentation gating, net position, and used 3+ distinct lifecycle statuses (active, final_completion differentiated by release eligibility), passing JE-U3-05. However, wo_skills also failed JE-U3-06 (no aging data). The scores are: wi_skills 5/7, wo_skills 6/7. Surprisingly, wo_skills performed slightly better overall due to richer status differentiation. The skill-assisted run had a well-structured approach but the skill's generate_report output was more limited than expected, missing the aged retention display and status granularity for final-completion subs. wo_skills produced a more complete report by implementing a custom Node.js solution that captured more of the required dimensions.
